metadata.title: Java MCQ Quiz
metadata.description: 100 multiple-choice questions covering Core Java, OOP, Collections, Multithreading, Exceptions, and Java 8+ features.

questions.question: What is Java?
options: A compiled, platform-dependent language like C++
A high-level, OOP, platform-independent language developed by James Gosling
A scripting language that runs only in browsers
A database query language used with Oracle
questions.explanation: Java is high-level, OOP, and platform-independent. Its bytecode + JVM enables 'write once, run anywhere'.

questions.question: Why is Java platform-independent?
options: It compiles directly to native machine code for each OS
The javac compiler produces bytecode that any JVM can execute
It uses C++ headers to ensure cross-platform compatibility
It relies on the operating system kernel for portability
questions.explanation: javac produces bytecode (.class), not machine code. Any machine with a compatible JVM runs the same bytecode.

questions.question: Which statement correctly describes JDK, JRE, and JVM?
options: JVM = JDK + JRE + tools
JRE = JVM + tools; JDK = JRE + standard libraries
JVM runs bytecode; JRE = JVM + libraries; JDK = JRE + compiler + tools
JDK and JRE are the same; JVM is a subset of JDK
questions.explanation: JVM runs bytecode. JRE = JVM + standard libraries (enough to run). JDK = JRE + javac + tools (needed to develop).

questions.question: Is Java a purely object-oriented language?
options: Yes, everything in Java is an object
Yes, because it supports all four OOP pillars
No, because it has primitive types (int, char, boolean, etc.) that are not objects
No, because it doesn't support multiple inheritance
questions.explanation: Java has 8 primitive types that are not objects. A purely OO language treats everything as an object.

questions.question: What is bytecode in Java?
options: Machine code specific to a particular processor
An intermediate, platform-neutral instruction set produced by the Java compiler
The source code written by the developer
Binary code stored directly in RAM
questions.explanation: Bytecode is an intermediate instruction set. The JVM either interprets it or JIT-compiles it to native code at runtime.

questions.question: What does the JIT compiler do?
options: Compiles Java source code to bytecode before execution
Validates bytecode for security vulnerabilities
Compiles frequently executed bytecode to native machine code at runtime
Links class files together at compile time
questions.explanation: Just-In-Time compiler compiles 'hot spots' (frequently run bytecode) into native code at runtime for better performance.

questions.question: What is the role of a ClassLoader in Java?
options: Compiles .java files to .class files
Loads class files into JVM memory on demand following parent-delegation
Manages garbage collection of unused classes
Provides JIT compilation for hot methods
questions.explanation: ClassLoader loads class files into memory on demand. Hierarchy: Bootstrap → Extension → Application loaders with parent-delegation.

questions.question: Which memory areas are managed by the JVM?
options: Only the Heap and Stack
Heap, Stack, and CPU cache
Heap, Stack, Method Area/Metaspace, PC Register, Native Method Stack
Code Segment, Data Segment, and BSS Segment
questions.explanation: JVM manages: Heap (objects), Stack (frames), Method Area/Metaspace (class metadata), PC Register, Native Method Stack.

questions.question: What is the key difference between Stack and Heap memory in Java?
options: Stack is for objects; Heap is for primitive values
Stack is thread-local for method frames/locals; Heap is shared across threads for objects
Both are thread-local; only their sizes differ
Heap is faster than Stack because it uses a cache-friendly layout
questions.explanation: Stack is thread-local and stores method frames and local variables. Heap is shared across threads and stores all objects.

questions.question: What is the difference between == and .equals() in Java?
options: Both compare object contents — they are interchangeable
== compares references (same object in memory); .equals() compares content/value
== compares content; .equals() compares memory addresses
.equals() is only available for String, not other classes
questions.explanation: == compares references. .equals() compares content as defined by the class (String and wrappers override it).

questions.question: Which is NOT a main feature of Java?
options: Platform independence
Pointer-based memory management
Multithreading support
Robustness and security
questions.explanation: Java deliberately omits pointers (uses references instead) for safety and simplicity. All others are core Java features.

questions.question: What is the default value of a local variable in Java?
options: 0 for numeric, false for boolean, null for references
null for all types
Local variables have no default value; they must be explicitly initialized
It depends on the JVM implementation
questions.explanation: Local variables have no default. The compiler enforces explicit initialization before use. Instance variables do get defaults.

questions.question: Can a Java program execute without a main() method?
options: Yes, using a static initializer block
Yes, if the class extends Thread
No, the JVM strictly requires main() as the entry point since Java 7
Yes, if the class implements Runnable
questions.explanation: Since Java 7 the JVM strictly requires main(). A static block could print output but main() was still needed.

questions.question: Which correctly distinguishes final, finally, and finalize()?
options: final=block for cleanup; finally=keyword for constants; finalize()=override method
All three are interchangeable ways to end execution
final=keyword (constant/no-override/no-extend); finally=cleanup block; finalize()=deprecated GC method
finalize()=block that always runs; final=GC hook; finally=constant keyword
questions.explanation: final: keyword. finally: block after try/catch. finalize(): deprecated method called by GC before reclaiming an object.

questions.question: What is a package in Java?
options: A compressed .jar archive of class files
A keyword for importing external libraries
A namespace that groups related classes/interfaces to avoid name clashes and enable access control
A module that encapsulates the entire JVM runtime
questions.explanation: A package is a namespace grouping related classes. Benefits: no name clashes, access control, better organization.

questions.question: What are the four pillars of OOP?
options: Compilation, Linking, Loading, Execution
Classes, Objects, Methods, Fields
Encapsulation, Inheritance, Polymorphism, Abstraction
Modularity, Reusability, Scalability, Portability
questions.explanation: The four OOP pillars are Encapsulation, Inheritance, Polymorphism, and Abstraction.

questions.question: What is encapsulation?
options: Hiding the entire class from other packages
Bundling data and behavior inside a class, restricting direct field access via getters/setters
Making all methods static so no object is needed
Inheriting all methods from a parent class
questions.explanation: Encapsulation bundles data + methods inside a class, using private fields with public getters/setters to restrict direct access.

questions.question: What does the 'extends' keyword enable in Java?
options: Multiple inheritance between classes
Inheritance: a subclass acquires properties and methods of a superclass
Interface implementation
Static method sharing between classes
questions.explanation: extends enables inheritance. Java supports single class inheritance and multiple interface inheritance.

questions.question: Why doesn't Java support multiple class inheritance?
options: To reduce compilation time
To avoid the diamond problem — ambiguity when two parents have the same method
Because JVM cannot handle more than one parent class pointer
To simplify the bytecode format
questions.explanation: The diamond problem causes ambiguity. Java solves this by allowing extends of one class but implements of multiple interfaces.

questions.question: Which best describes polymorphism?
options: An object having multiple constructors
The ability to store different types in the same variable
One interface, many implementations — compile-time (overloading) and runtime (overriding)
Inheriting methods from multiple parent classes
questions.explanation: Polymorphism = one interface, many implementations. Compile-time via overloading; runtime via overriding with dynamic dispatch.

questions.question: What is the key difference between method overloading and overriding?
options: Overloading changes return type; overriding changes parameter list
Overloading: same name, different params, same class, compile-time. Overriding: subclass redefines parent method, runtime
Both are resolved at runtime using dynamic dispatch
Overriding requires the static keyword; overloading requires the final keyword
questions.explanation: Overloading: same name, different parameters, compile-time resolution. Overriding: subclass redefines parent method, runtime via virtual dispatch.

questions.question: Which statement about abstract classes vs interfaces is correct?
options: An abstract class can be instantiated; an interface cannot
An interface can have constructors; an abstract class cannot
Abstract class: may have concrete methods + fields, single inheritance. Interface: multiple inheritance, default methods since Java 8
Both abstract classes and interfaces are identical since Java 8
questions.explanation: Abstract class can have concrete methods, fields, constructors, single inheritance. Interface: abstract + default/static methods, multiple inheritance.

questions.question: Which access modifiers can a TOP-LEVEL class have?
options: public, private, protected, or package-private
Only public or private
Only public or package-private (no modifier)
public, protected, or package-private
questions.explanation: A top-level class can only be public or package-private. Inner/nested classes can also be private or protected.

questions.question: What is a constructor in Java?
options: A method that returns the class name
A special block with the same name as the class, called on object creation to initialize state
A static method that creates objects
An interface method that must be overridden
questions.explanation: A constructor has the same name as the class, no return type, is called on object creation, and initializes the object's state.

questions.question: What is a copy constructor?
options: A constructor provided automatically by the compiler in Java
A constructor that takes another instance of the same class to duplicate its state
A constructor that copies bytes from a file into an object
A constructor that clones the JVM heap
questions.explanation: A copy constructor takes another instance of the same class to create a duplicate. Java doesn't provide one automatically — you write it.

questions.question: Can a constructor be declared as final, static, or abstract?
options: Yes, all three are valid constructor modifiers
Only static is valid for constructors
No — constructors cannot be final, static, or abstract
Only abstract is valid for constructors in abstract classes
questions.explanation: Constructors aren't inherited (final meaningless), operate on instances (static wrong), and must have a body (abstract wrong).

questions.question: What does the 'this' keyword refer to in Java?
options: The parent class of the current object
A static reference to the class itself
A reference to the current object instance
The thread that created the object
questions.explanation: this refers to the current object. Used to access instance fields, call other constructors via this(), or pass the current object.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the 'super' keyword?
options: Creates a superclass instance independently
References the immediate parent class for methods, fields, or constructor invocation
Makes a method accessible to all subclasses
Marks a method as overridable in child classes
questions.explanation: super references the immediate parent class — super.method(), super.field, super() for parent constructor.

questions.question: What does the 'static' keyword mean in Java?
options: The member can only be accessed at compile time
The member belongs to instances only, not the class
The member belongs to the class, shared across all instances, loaded once with the class
The member is immutable and cannot be reassigned
questions.explanation: static marks a field/method/block as belonging to the class, not instances. Shared across all objects, loaded once.

questions.question: Can we override a static method in Java?
options: Yes, static methods are overridden just like instance methods
Yes, but only in abstract classes
No — static methods are hidden in subclasses, not overridden; polymorphism does not apply
No — static methods cannot be inherited at all
questions.explanation: Static methods belong to the class, not instances. They are hidden (not overridden) in subclasses. No runtime polymorphism applies.

questions.question: Why are Strings immutable in Java?
options: To save memory by limiting how many Strings can be created
For security, thread safety, and String Pool optimization (same literal can be shared)
Because the JVM does not allow mutable objects
To enforce the use of StringBuilder instead
questions.explanation: Immutability ensures security (passwords/URLs safe), thread safety (no sync needed), and String Pool optimization (shared literals).

questions.question: What is the String Pool?
options: A thread pool specifically for String operations
A special heap region where String literals are stored and reused across references
A data structure that sorts strings alphabetically
A cache in the Method Area that stores compiled regex patterns
questions.explanation: The String Pool is a special heap region. String s = "hello" twice makes both references point to the same pool object.

questions.question: Which is the correct comparison of String, StringBuilder, and StringBuffer?
options: All three are immutable; they differ only in performance
String: mutable; StringBuilder: immutable fast; StringBuffer: immutable thread-safe
String: immutable; StringBuilder: mutable not thread-safe fast; StringBuffer: mutable thread-safe slower
StringBuilder and StringBuffer are identical; String is slower than both
questions.explanation: String is immutable. StringBuilder is mutable and fast (single-threaded). StringBuffer is mutable, synchronized, slower.

questions.question: What is the difference between String s = "abc" and String s = new String("abc")?
options: There is no difference — both produce the same result
The literal form uses the String Pool; new String() creates a heap object (possibly two objects total)
new String() is more memory efficient because it skips the pool
The literal form always creates a new object on every assignment
questions.explanation: Literal reuses/creates a pool entry. new String() always creates a heap object in addition to the pool entry — potentially two objects.

questions.question: How should you compare two String values for content equality?
options: Use == operator for the most reliable comparison
Use .compareTo() for all equality checks
Use .equals() for content comparison; never use == for this purpose
Use String.compare() static method
questions.explanation: Use .equals() for content, .equalsIgnoreCase() for case-insensitive, .compareTo() for lexicographic order. == checks references only.

questions.question: What does String.intern() do?
options: Converts a String to an internal byte array
Returns the canonical pool representation — adds to pool if absent
Creates an immutable view of the String
Compresses the string to save heap memory
questions.explanation: intern() returns the String Pool canonical form. If the string is already pooled, returns that reference; otherwise adds and returns it.

questions.question: Is String a primitive type in Java?
options: Yes, String is a primitive type like int and boolean
Yes, but only for string literals declared with double quotes
No, String is an object — java.lang.String
It depends on how the string is declared
questions.explanation: String is an object (java.lang.String). Java has no primitive string type. The 8 primitives are: byte, short, int, long, float, double, char, boolean.

questions.question: From which Java version can String be used in a switch statement?
options: Java 5 — when generics were introduced
Java 6
Java 7
Java 9 — as part of the module system
questions.explanation: String support in switch was added in Java 7. Before that, switch only supported int-compatible primitives and enums.

questions.question: What is the output of: System.out.println("10" + 20 + 30)?
options: "10" + 20 + 30 is a compile error
questions.explanation: Evaluated left-to-right: "10"+20 → "1020" (string concat), then "1020"+30 → "102030".

questions.question: What is the easiest way to reverse a String in Java?
options: String.reverse(s)
new StringBuilder(s).reverse().toString()
Collections.reverse(s.toCharArray())
s.split("").sort().join()
questions.explanation: new StringBuilder(s).reverse().toString() is the simplest approach. Alternatively, iterate from end to start manually.

questions.question: What is the Java Collections Framework?
options: A set of design patterns for building large applications
A unified architecture of interfaces, implementations, and algorithms for storing and manipulating groups of objects
A thread management library introduced in Java 5
A serialization framework for persisting objects
questions.explanation: JCF is a unified architecture. Core interfaces: Collection, List, Set, Queue, Map (Map doesn't extend Collection by design).

questions.question: Why doesn't Map extend the Collection interface?
options: Because Map was added later in Java 2 without refactoring
Because Map is a key-value structure and doesn't fit the single-element Collection contract
For performance reasons — Map operations are O(1)
Because Map already extends Iterable directly
questions.explanation: A Map holds key-value pairs. The Collection contract (add(E), remove(E)) doesn't naturally apply to a key-value structure.

questions.question: When should you prefer LinkedList over ArrayList?
options: When you need fast random access by index
When you need frequent insertions/deletions at the head or tail and rarely access by index
When you need thread safety without synchronization
When you need to sort elements automatically
questions.explanation: LinkedList: O(1) insertions at head/tail, O(n) random access. ArrayList: O(1) random access, slower mid-list insertions.

questions.question: What is the main difference between ArrayList and Vector?
options: Vector allows null elements; ArrayList does not
ArrayList is part of JCF; Vector is not a collection
Vector is synchronized (thread-safe but slower) and legacy; ArrayList is not synchronized (fast)
ArrayList doubles capacity on resize; Vector grows by 50%
questions.explanation: Vector is synchronized and legacy. ArrayList is not synchronized — use CopyOnWriteArrayList or Collections.synchronizedList() for concurrency.

questions.question: Which is true about HashMap vs Hashtable?
options: Hashtable allows null keys; HashMap does not
HashMap and Hashtable are both synchronized with identical performance
HashMap: not synchronized, allows one null key; Hashtable: synchronized, no null keys/values, legacy
HashMap is slower because it performs additional null checks
questions.explanation: HashMap is unsynchronized and allows one null key. Hashtable is synchronized, no nulls, legacy — prefer ConcurrentHashMap.

questions.question: How does HashMap work internally?
options: It uses a sorted array of key-value pairs with binary search
It uses an array of buckets; hashCode() picks the bucket; equals() resolves collisions; Java 8+ uses red-black tree for large buckets
It uses a doubly linked list and sequential search for all lookups
It maintains elements in insertion order using a linked array
questions.explanation: HashMap uses an array of buckets (default 16). hashCode() picks bucket. Collisions stored as linked list — or red-black tree (>8 entries) since Java 8.

questions.question: What is the default load factor of a HashMap?
options: 0.5 — map rehashes when 50% full
0.75 — rehashing triggered when map is 75% full
1.0 — rehashing only when completely full
0.9 — to minimize wasted space
questions.explanation: Default load factor is 0.75. When the map is 75% full, the internal array doubles. Balances space vs time efficiency.

questions.question: What happens when two HashMap keys have the same hashCode?
options: The second key replaces the first key automatically
An exception is thrown — keys must have unique hash codes
Collision occurs — both entries share the same bucket in a linked list (or tree in Java 8+)
The map rehashes immediately to resolve the conflict
questions.explanation: Collision: both entries land in the same bucket, stored as a linked list. equals() resolves the exact key on lookup.

questions.question: What is the contract between hashCode() and equals()?
options: If a.hashCode() == b.hashCode(), then a.equals(b) must be true
equals() and hashCode() are independent — no relationship required
If a.equals(b) is true, then a.hashCode() == b.hashCode() must be true
Objects with different hashCodes are always unequal
questions.explanation: Contract: equals true → same hashCode (required). Same hashCode does NOT imply equals. Breaking this breaks all hash-based collections.

questions.question: What is the correct ordering behavior of LinkedHashMap vs TreeMap?
options: LinkedHashMap sorts by key; TreeMap maintains insertion order
Both maintain insertion order but with different performance
LinkedHashMap maintains insertion/access order; TreeMap sorts keys by natural order or Comparator
TreeMap and LinkedHashMap are identical in ordering behavior
questions.explanation: LinkedHashMap: insertion (or access) order, O(1). TreeMap: sorted by keys (natural or Comparator), O(log n), no null keys.

questions.question: What is the main difference between HashSet and TreeSet?
options: HashSet maintains insertion order; TreeSet does not
TreeSet is backed by HashMap; HashSet is backed by TreeMap
HashSet: unordered O(1) backed by HashMap; TreeSet: sorted O(log n) backed by TreeMap
Both are identical in behavior but differ in thread safety
questions.explanation: HashSet: unordered, O(1), backed by HashMap, allows one null. TreeSet: sorted, O(log n), backed by TreeMap, no null.

questions.question: What does ListIterator offer over Iterator?
options: ListIterator works on any Collection; Iterator only works on Lists
ListIterator is thread-safe; Iterator is not
ListIterator is bidirectional and supports add()/set(); Iterator is forward-only
ListIterator provides fail-safe iteration; Iterator is fail-fast
questions.explanation: ListIterator: bidirectional, works only on Lists, supports add() and set() during iteration. Iterator: forward-only, works on any Collection.

questions.question: What is the difference between fail-fast and fail-safe iterators?
options: Fail-fast is for sorted collections; fail-safe is for unsorted collections
Fail-fast throws ConcurrentModificationException on concurrent modification; fail-safe iterates a copy without exception
Both throw exceptions but fail-safe retries automatically
Fail-safe uses synchronization; fail-fast uses CAS operations
questions.explanation: Fail-fast (ArrayList, HashMap): throws ConcurrentModificationException. Fail-safe (ConcurrentHashMap, CopyOnWriteArrayList): iterates a copy.

questions.question: What is the difference between Comparable and Comparator?
options: Comparable is for numbers only; Comparator is for Strings only
Both define the same interface — Comparable is just an older name
Comparable: natural ordering in the class via compareTo(). Comparator: external, separate class/lambda, multiple sort orders
Comparator is deprecated since Java 8; use Comparable with lambdas instead
questions.explanation: Comparable: class implements natural order via compareTo(), single order. Comparator: external class/lambda via compare(), multiple orders.

questions.question: How does ConcurrentHashMap improve on Hashtable?
options: ConcurrentHashMap locks the entire map; Hashtable locks individual buckets
ConcurrentHashMap uses bucket-level CAS and synchronization (Java 8+), not full-map locking like Hashtable
ConcurrentHashMap is not thread-safe; it just avoids Hashtable's overhead
Both use the same locking strategy with different default capacities
questions.explanation: Java 8+ ConcurrentHashMap uses fine-grained CAS + per-bucket sync. Hashtable locks the entire map — far worse concurrency.

questions.question: When is CopyOnWriteArrayList the best choice?
options: When writes are very frequent and reads are rare
When you need a sorted thread-safe list
When reads are very frequent and writes are rare — it creates a fresh array copy on every write
When you need a blocking list with producer-consumer semantics
questions.explanation: CopyOnWriteArrayList creates a fresh array on every write — great for read-heavy rarely-written scenarios.

questions.question: What is a BlockingQueue used for?
options: A Queue that sorts elements before dequeuing
A Queue that throws exceptions when empty or full
A Queue that blocks on put() when full and take() when empty — foundation of producer-consumer pattern
A Queue backed by a database for persistent messaging
questions.explanation: BlockingQueue blocks on put() when full and take() when empty. Implementations: ArrayBlockingQueue, LinkedBlockingQueue.

questions.question: What does PriorityQueue guarantee?
options: FIFO order — first in, first out
LIFO order — last in, first out
The head is always the highest-priority (minimum) element; O(log n) insert/remove
All elements are sorted in descending order
questions.explanation: PriorityQueue head = minimum element (natural order or Comparator). O(log n) insert and remove. Does NOT guarantee full sort.

questions.question: Can you store primitive types (like int) directly in a Java collection?
options: Yes, primitives are stored directly for performance
Yes, but only in specialized primitive collections
No — primitives are autoboxed to wrapper types (int → Integer) when added to collections
No — collections only store references to String objects
questions.explanation: Collections only hold objects. Primitives are autoboxed: int→Integer, double→Double, etc. Unboxing happens on retrieval.

questions.question: How do you create an unmodifiable (read-only) collection in Java?
options: By declaring the collection variable final
By using Collections.unmodifiableList() or List.of() (Java 9+)
By using synchronized collection wrappers
By calling collection.lock() before passing it
questions.explanation: Collections.unmodifiable*() wraps existing collections. List.of()/Map.of()/Set.of() (Java 9+) create truly immutable instances.

questions.question: What is an exception in Java?
options: A compile error that prevents bytecode generation
An abnormal runtime event that disrupts normal program flow, represented as a Throwable object
A warning logged to stderr when a method is used incorrectly
A type of comment for documenting error-prone code
questions.explanation: An exception is an abnormal event that disrupts normal flow. Java represents them as Throwable (or its subclasses) objects.

questions.question: What is the correct Java exception hierarchy?
options: Exception → Throwable → Error
Throwable → Exception → Error
Throwable → Error (unrecoverable) and Exception → checked or RuntimeException (unchecked)
Error → Exception → RuntimeException → Throwable
questions.explanation: Throwable splits into Error (unrecoverable, e.g. OutOfMemoryError) and Exception (recoverable → checked and unchecked/RuntimeException).

questions.question: What defines a checked exception?
options: An exception that extends RuntimeException
An exception verified at runtime, not requiring a catch or declaration
An exception verified at compile time that must be caught or declared with throws
Any exception thrown inside a synchronized block
questions.explanation: Checked exceptions are verified at compile time. You MUST catch or declare them. Examples: IOException, SQLException.

questions.question: What is the difference between throw and throws?
options: throw declares exceptions in a method signature; throws actually throws an instance
throw actually throws an exception instance; throws declares what exceptions a method may throw
Both are identical — they can be used interchangeably
throws is used only for unchecked exceptions; throw is for checked exceptions
questions.explanation: throw: actually throws an exception (throw new IOException()). throws: method signature declaration of potential exceptions.

questions.question: When does a finally block NOT execute?
options: When the try block catches an exception
When there is a return statement in the try block
When System.exit() is called, a JVM crash occurs, or an infinite loop runs inside try
When the method is overridden in a subclass
questions.explanation: finally always runs EXCEPT: JVM exits (System.exit()), JVM crash, infinite loop/deadlock inside try, or thread is forcibly killed.

questions.question: Is try-finally without a catch block valid Java?
options: No — every try block must have at least one catch
Yes — try-finally is valid; catch is optional
No — try must always be paired with both catch and finally
Yes — but only for checked exceptions
questions.explanation: try-finally is perfectly valid. catch is not required. Also, try-with-resources doesn't need explicit catch.

questions.question: What is try-with-resources?
options: A try block that automatically imports external modules
A syntax (Java 7+) that auto-closes AutoCloseable resources — no explicit finally needed
A way to retry failed operations inside a catch block
A mechanism to catch multiple exceptions in one block
questions.explanation: try-with-resources auto-closes any resource implementing AutoCloseable. Cleaner and safer than manual close in finally.

questions.question: How do you catch multiple exception types in a single catch block (Java 7+)?
options: catch (Exception e1, Exception e2)
catch ([IOException, SQLException] e)
catch (IOException | SQLException e)
catch (IOException & SQLException e)
questions.explanation: Multi-catch syntax: catch (IOException | SQLException e). The variable e is implicitly final. Available since Java 7.

questions.question: What happens if a finally block has a return statement?
options: Compile error — return is not allowed in finally
The finally return is ignored; the try's return takes effect
The finally return overrides the try's return value (bad practice)
An exception is thrown about conflicting return statements
questions.explanation: If finally has return, it overrides try's return. This is considered bad practice and static analyzers warn against it.

questions.question: How do you create a custom checked exception?
options: Extend Throwable and mark it @Checked
Extend RuntimeException to create a checked exception
Extend Exception (for checked) or RuntimeException (for unchecked)
Implement the CheckedException interface
questions.explanation: For checked: extend Exception. For unchecked: extend RuntimeException. Add meaningful constructors that delegate to super.

questions.question: What is the difference between a process and a thread?
options: A process is lightweight; a thread has its own JVM
Both share the same memory space — they differ only in priority
A process has its own memory space; a thread is lightweight and shares the heap within a process
Threads can run on multiple CPUs; processes cannot
questions.explanation: Process: independent program with own memory. Thread: lightweight unit of execution inside a process, sharing the heap but with its own stack.

questions.question: What is the preferred way to create a thread in Java?
options: Extend Thread and override run() — it gives direct JVM access
Implement Runnable and pass it to Thread — keeps class hierarchy free for other uses
Always use the ThreadGroup class for managed threads
Use Thread.spawn() factory method for thread creation
questions.explanation: Implementing Runnable is preferred: keeps inheritance free, works with lambdas, and integrates with ExecutorService.

questions.question: What is the difference between calling start() and run() on a Thread?
options: Both create a new thread; start() is just the older API
start() creates a new thread and calls run() on it; calling run() directly executes on the current thread
run() is the asynchronous version; start() executes synchronously
There is no difference — start() and run() are aliases
questions.explanation: start() creates a new thread. Calling run() directly just executes the code in the current thread — no concurrency at all.

questions.question: What are the valid thread states in Java?
options: CREATED, RUNNING, PAUSED, STOPPED
ALIVE, SLEEPING, WAITING, DEAD
NEW, RUNNABLE, BLOCKED, WAITING, TIMED_WAITING, TERMINATED
INIT, ACTIVE, BLOCKED, FINISHED
questions.explanation: Java thread states: NEW → RUNNABLE → (BLOCKED / WAITING / TIMED_WAITING) → TERMINATED.

questions.question: What does the synchronized keyword do in Java?
options: It prevents a method from being overridden in subclasses
It makes a variable visible across all threads immediately
It ensures only one thread at a time can execute a protected method or block
It compiles a method to native code for faster execution
questions.explanation: synchronized locks on an instance, class, or object. Only one thread executes the protected code at a time — mutual exclusion.

questions.question: What does the volatile keyword guarantee?
options: Atomicity — volatile operations complete without interruption
Both visibility and atomicity for compound operations like i++
Visibility only — a write by one thread is immediately visible to others; no atomicity guarantee
Thread priority — volatile threads run before non-volatile threads
questions.explanation: volatile guarantees visibility (write immediately visible to all threads) but NOT atomicity. Use AtomicInteger for atomic compound ops.

questions.question: Which correctly describes wait(), sleep(), and yield()?
options: All three release the lock held by the current thread
wait(): releases lock, waits on object monitor; sleep(): pauses, NO lock release; yield(): hints scheduler to pause
sleep() releases lock; wait() does not; yield() terminates the thread
All three are identical except for their timeout parameters
questions.explanation: wait(): releases lock, on object monitor, inside synchronized. sleep(): pauses, NO lock release. yield(): hint to scheduler, no guarantee.

questions.question: What is a deadlock?
options: When a thread runs out of memory and cannot allocate objects
When two or more threads wait forever for locks held by each other, forming a cycle
When a high-priority thread starves lower-priority threads indefinitely
When a thread throws an exception inside a synchronized block
questions.explanation: Deadlock: threads A and B each hold a lock the other needs — circular wait. Prevention: fixed lock ordering, timeouts, avoiding nested locks.

questions.question: What is a race condition?
options: A competition between threads to acquire the CPU first
Multiple threads accessing shared state without proper synchronization, leading to unpredictable results
Two threads running at exactly the same speed simultaneously
A condition where Thread.sleep() causes timing issues
questions.explanation: Race condition: multiple threads access/modify shared state without sync, leading to data corruption and unpredictable results.

questions.question: What is the benefit of using ExecutorService over manually creating threads?
options: ExecutorService automatically fixes race conditions
It provides a thread pool that reuses threads, reducing overhead vs creating new threads for every task
ExecutorService is faster because it uses native OS threads
It prevents all forms of deadlock automatically
questions.explanation: ExecutorService (Java 5) manages a thread pool, reusing threads for tasks. Avoids the overhead of creating/destroying threads per task.

questions.question: What is the difference between Runnable and Callable?
options: Runnable is for threads; Callable is for processes
Both return values; Callable additionally supports exception handling
Runnable.run() returns void and can't throw checked exceptions; Callable.call() returns a value and can throw checked exceptions
Callable is deprecated since Java 8; use Runnable with lambdas instead
questions.explanation: Runnable: run() → void, no checked exceptions. Callable<V>: call() → V, can throw checked exceptions. Callable pairs with Future.

questions.question: How does CompletableFuture differ from Future?
options: CompletableFuture is synchronous; Future is asynchronous
Future supports callbacks; CompletableFuture uses blocking get()
Future: placeholder with blocking get(); CompletableFuture: non-blocking, composable async pipelines (thenApply, thenCompose)
Both are identical since Java 9 unified them
questions.explanation: Future: blocking get(). CompletableFuture (Java 8): non-blocking, composable via thenApply/thenCompose/thenCombine, supports async callbacks.

questions.question: What does the Java Memory Model (JMM) define?
options: The maximum heap size for Java applications
How the JVM allocates objects in different memory regions
How threads interact through memory — what writes are visible to what reads; happens-before relationships
The garbage collection algorithm used by the JVM
questions.explanation: JMM defines thread interaction through memory: visibility guarantees, happens-before rules, effects of volatile/synchronized/final.

questions.question: What is the key difference between CountDownLatch and CyclicBarrier?
options: CountDownLatch is reusable; CyclicBarrier is one-shot
Both are identical — only their method names differ
CountDownLatch: one-shot, threads wait until count reaches zero. CyclicBarrier: reusable, all threads wait at a barrier until all arrive
CyclicBarrier blocks the main thread; CountDownLatch blocks worker threads
questions.explanation: CountDownLatch: one-shot, not reusable. CyclicBarrier: trips when all threads arrive, resets and can be reused.

questions.question: How do classes like AtomicInteger achieve thread safety?
options: By using synchronized methods internally
By using volatile variables for all fields
By using CAS (Compare-And-Swap) hardware instructions — lock-free atomic updates
By creating a new copy of the value for each thread
questions.explanation: Atomic classes use CAS (Compare-And-Swap) operations — hardware-level atomic instructions — for lock-free thread-safe updates.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature introduced in Java 8?
options: Lambda expressions and functional interfaces
Stream API for declarative data processing
Record classes and sealed interfaces
Optional class and new Date/Time API
questions.explanation: Records and sealed interfaces came in Java 14/16/17. Java 8 introduced: lambdas, streams, Optional, default methods, new Date/Time API.

questions.question: What is a lambda expression in Java?
options: A special type of for-loop for iterating collections
An anonymous class with multiple abstract methods
A concise anonymous function that can be assigned to a functional interface
A compiler directive that inlines method calls
questions.explanation: A lambda is a concise, anonymous function: (params) -> expression. Can only be assigned to a functional interface.

questions.question: What defines a functional interface?
options: An interface that has exactly two abstract methods
An interface with no default methods and any number of abstract methods
An interface with exactly one abstract method (default/static methods don't count); annotated @FunctionalInterface
An interface that extends java.util.function.Function
questions.explanation: Functional interface: exactly one abstract method. Default/static methods don't count. @FunctionalInterface is optional but enforces this at compile time.

questions.question: Which statement best describes the Stream API?
options: A replacement for InputStream/OutputStream for file I/O
A data structure that stores elements like an array but is thread-safe
A declarative pipeline for processing sequences of elements — filter, map, reduce — that is lazy and supports parallelism
A JDBC abstraction for streaming query results
questions.explanation: Stream API: declarative processing (filter/map/reduce), lazy evaluation, consumes a source (collection/array), and supports parallel execution.

questions.question: What is the difference between intermediate and terminal Stream operations?
options: Intermediate operations execute immediately; terminal operations are lazy
Terminal operations return a new Stream; intermediate operations produce results
Intermediate operations are lazy and return a new Stream; terminal operations trigger execution and produce a result
Both types execute when the stream is created
questions.explanation: Intermediate (filter, map, sorted): lazy, return a new stream. Terminal (collect, forEach, reduce, count): trigger execution, produce results.

questions.question: What is the difference between Stream.map() and Stream.flatMap()?
options: map() is for numbers; flatMap() is for Strings
Both perform the same transformation; flatMap() is just the parallel version
map(): 1-to-1 element transformation. flatMap(): 1-to-many, flattens resulting streams into a single stream
flatMap() applies the function twice; map() applies it once
questions.explanation: map: applies function to each element (1-to-1). flatMap: applies function returning a stream, then flattens all into one stream (1-to-many).

questions.question: What is the purpose of Optional in Java 8?
options: To mark a method parameter as not required
A container for a possibly-null value that forces explicit absence handling to avoid NullPointerException
A thread-safe wrapper for shared mutable values
A lazy collection that evaluates values only when accessed
questions.explanation: Optional wraps a possibly-null value. Use isPresent(), orElse(), map(), ifPresent() to handle absence explicitly — avoids NPE.

questions.question: What is a method reference and what does String::toUpperCase represent?
options: A static import of the String class; toUpperCase is a field
A shorthand lambda for calling a method — String::toUpperCase is equivalent to s -> s.toUpperCase()
A way to access String's private toUpperCase implementation
A compile-time constant representing the method signature
questions.explanation: Method reference is shorthand for a lambda. String::toUpperCase = s -> s.toUpperCase(). Four kinds: static, bound, unbound instance, constructor.

questions.question: What are default methods in interfaces, and why were they introduced?
options: Methods that are automatically called when no override exists, introduced to replace abstract classes
Methods with a body inside an interface; introduced to add methods to existing interfaces without breaking implementations
Private methods in interfaces that enforce access control
Static utility methods that belong to the interface itself
questions.explanation: Default methods (Java 8): interface methods with a body. Introduced so new methods (e.g., List.forEach) could be added without breaking existing implementations.

questions.question: What is the key difference between a Collection and a Stream?
options: Collection is lazy; Stream stores all elements eagerly
Both store data; Stream additionally supports parallel operations
Collection stores data in memory; Stream is a processing pipeline — lazy, single-use, doesn't store data
Stream is a subtype of Collection with extra processing methods
questions.explanation: Collection: stores elements in memory. Stream: processing pipeline over data, lazy, single-use, doesn't store elements, supports parallelism.

questions.question: What is serialization in Java?
options: Converting source code to bytecode using javac
Converting an object into a byte stream for storage or transmission; deserialization reverses it
Compressing class files to reduce JAR size
Converting XML data into Java objects
questions.explanation: Serialization: object → byte stream. Class must implement Serializable. transient skips fields. serialVersionUID handles version compatibility.

questions.question: What is the difference between a shallow copy and a deep copy?
options: Shallow copy is faster than deep copy but produces the same result
Both types produce independent copies — they differ only in implementation
Shallow copy shares nested object references; deep copy recursively copies nested objects for full independence
Deep copy only works for immutable objects; shallow copy works for all objects
questions.explanation: Shallow copy: nested object references are shared. Deep copy: recursively copies nested objects — fully independent duplicate.

questions.question: What is type erasure in Java generics?
options: A runtime feature that removes objects from the heap when no longer needed
The process where generic type information is stripped at runtime for backward compatibility — List<String> becomes List
A compile-time check that prevents incorrect types from being added to collections
A refactoring tool that automatically removes unused type parameters
questions.explanation: Type erasure: at runtime, generic info is stripped (List<String> → List) for backward compatibility with pre-generics code.

questions.question: Can you force garbage collection in Java?
options: Yes, System.gc() immediately collects all unreachable objects
Yes, calling Runtime.freeMemory() triggers a full GC cycle
You can suggest GC via System.gc() but the JVM decides — GC cannot be forced
No, Java has no API for interacting with the garbage collector
questions.explanation: System.gc() and Runtime.gc() are hints — the JVM decides when to collect. GC cannot be forced. Modern GCs: Serial, Parallel, G1, ZGC.

questions.question: If you override equals() in a class, what else MUST you also override?
options: toString() — for consistent debugging output
hashCode() — to maintain the equals-hashCode contract for hash-based collections
clone() — to allow object duplication
compareTo() — to enable sorting
questions.explanation: Contract: if a.equals(b) → a.hashCode() == b.hashCode(). Breaking this breaks HashMap, HashSet, and all hash-based collections.
